core_takeaway: The first speaker helped set the baseline and secured Jewel, one of Agent_1's 28-point top items, but the last speaker secured Apple, Agent_5's 35-point top item. Because every agent proposed and accepted the same baseline, position order did not create an advantage large enough to overcome the value-scale gap.
why_or_counterexample: This is a counterexample to a simple first-position advantage: Agent_1 got a preferred item through the consensus frame, yet Agent_5's last-position allocation was worth more. The decisive mechanism was not proposal order but a mutually acceptable one-item-per-agent baseline that preserved Quill for Agent_2 and let Agent_5 keep Apple.

phase_notes.public_discussion: The public discussion converged from Agent_1's Jewel/Pencil framing to a shared baseline: Jewel to Agent_1, Pencil to Agent_3, Quill to Agent_2, Stone to Agent_4, Apple to Agent_5. Agent_5 entered last but claimed Apple, his 35-point top item.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present. All five private-thinking entries treat the baseline as the plan, with Quill protection and Apple/Stone leverage making the allocation stable rather than first-speaker-favoring.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All five formal proposals are identical, so proposal authorship and order produced no differential allocation.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present. All 25 private votes accept; voters justify acceptance by their own secured item or by the shared two-round baseline.
phase_notes.reflection: Absent; the row lists zero reflection entries and the transcript has no reflection section.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 received Jewel, tied for Agent_1's top at 28, but Agent_5 received Apple, worth 35. The final utilities therefore favor the last position by 7 despite Agent_1 getting a top item.

evidence.quote: Jewel and Pencil (both worth 28 to me)

mechanisms.explanation: By the formal proposal phase, all agents submitted the same allocation and all proposals received unanimous acceptance. The first proposal had no unique advantage because the last proposal encoded the same deal and became the unanimously accepted result.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker got agenda influence but not payoff dominance
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's early framing around Jewel/Pencil and a two-step package helped make Jewel-to-Agent_1 part of the baseline. That is a real first-position benefit, but it only secured 28 utility and did not beat Agent_5's Apple payoff.

evidence.quote: secure one of Jewel or Pencil now
assigns Jewel to me (my top priority, value 28)

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker claimed a compatible high-value item
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 did not need to displace Agent_1 from Jewel or challenge the Jewel/Pencil coalition. Agent_5's desired Apple fit the consensus baseline and was worth more to Agent_5 than Jewel was to Agent_1.

evidence.quote: Top value items for me: Apple (35) first

mechanisms.theme: Quill protection anchored the deal more than position
mechanisms.explanation: The strongest constraint came from agents insisting Quill remain untouched, especially Agent_2 with a 64-point Quill value. The consensus satisfied that constraint, which made the baseline acceptable independent of who moved first.

evidence.quote: I will not trade Quill in Round 1
with Quill remaining out of Round 1 moves

mechanisms.theme: Private reasoning reinforced stability over first-mover extraction
mechanisms.explanation: The raw private-thinking entries show agents converging internally on the same lock-in baseline and Round 2 leverage story. That private convergence explains why Agent_1's early position produced agreement but not an outsized first-position payoff.

evidence.quote: The prevailing baseline is widely supported
